item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a fascinating time capsule from a time where SCPs were less so stories and moreso "Attempts at scientific reporting"… complete with silly expungements that add nothing to the reading experience. "█7%" genuinely sounds like a joke you'd see in a -J nowadays.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one, but have a few botanical nitpicks. I don't think a taproot could be hollow and full of digestive juices because they would keep it from being able to transport water and nutrients to the rest of the plant. The trunk could probably get big enough that it wouldn't need the extra space anyway. Also, plants aren't usually carnivorous because they need the calories or are bad at photosynthesizing; normally it's because they grow in nitrogen poor soil and need the proteins from animals to make up for it. This plant would probably already need more nitrogen than usual to make proteins so it can move, and even more so since its in a desert (poor soil), so that would definitely be a reasonable explanation here. Other than that though, this is a neat one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep! It's also the name of a mythical man-eating tree, which was the original inspiration for this skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't "Ya te veo" mean "I already see you" or something like that in Spanish?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Author of 2815 here.

I'm really pleased with this crosslink. It makes me think that 1001 and 2815 may be a portion of one big global root system, which makes it seem much more dangerous than they appear on their own. That would explain where all the organs are coming from. I'll be leaving that connection at this, however. This seems much more mysterious and unsettling when it is merely implied, and that nobody else knows of it in-universe.

It adds strength to both articles, IMO. Good job!

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So here we have a plant with some pretty heavy religious undertones (if the final paragraphs are to be believed, and reminded me a bit of the tree featured in SCP-2815 in that respect. With the redactions present in this article, there is plenty of wiggle room to slip a non-invasive crosslink in. As such, I am proposing the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1001 is incapable of digesting hard tissues completely: the bones of its prey are gradually excreted through channels near the top edges of the caudex. Once excreted, these bones are invariably picked up with one of the object’s leaves and moved to a location on or beneath the soil surface surrounding SCP-1001. The object arranges the bones in complex patterns which are demonstrably useless for water capture and, being primarily subterranean, do not aid in attracting prey. Their resemblance to the ███ █████ █████ Nazca [DATA REDACTED] and the devotional mandalas of SCP-[DATA REDACTED] have led Dr. Glastonbury to hypothesize that they may be representational or even [DATA REDACTED] anomalous effects.

To this:

SCP-1001 is incapable of digesting hard tissues completely: the bones of its prey are gradually excreted through channels near the top edges of the caudex. Once excreted, these bones are invariably picked up with one of the object’s leaves and moved to a location on or beneath the soil surface surrounding SCP-1001. The object arranges the bones in complex patterns which are demonstrably useless for water capture and, being primarily subterranean, do not aid in attracting prey. Their resemblance to the ███ █████ █████ Nazca [DATA REDACTED] and the devotional mandalas of SCP-[DATA REDACTED] have led Dr. Glastonbury to hypothesize that they may be representational or even [DATA REDACTED] anomalous effects.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this SCP quite a bit, but the data expungement is just way too much to give a good hook to the article.
Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No object capable of producing hazardous sound (sonic weaponry, audio-based memetic effects, high-decibel infrasound, etc.) is permitted within hearing distance of Bio Site-103.

I like this detail, but I have a slight nitpick. I think "hearing distance" should be changed to some sort of measurable distance. Hearing distance is subjective (i.e. some animals can hear father than humans, while some have a lower range but higher sensitivity, ect).
If this plant's adapted for audio predatory patterns and has been known to mimic things it hears, I feel like The Foundation would establish a distance limit with perhaps a 'buffer zone' 5m futher out than that, just for safety measures.

Despite my nitpick, this gets an upvote from me. I like the idea and the article. My only issue is the 'hearing distance' is early on in the article, and more picky readers may not even bother reading the rest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it would be really easy to add an image to this article. It wouldn't be hard and in my opinion flush out the article. An image can make a big difference in an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this touch a lot.

No object capable of producing hazardous sound (sonic weaponry, audio-based memetic effects, high-decibel infrasound, etc.) is permitted within hearing distance of Bio Site-103.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you have specific critiques re: "doesn't make much sense in some places" (other than the squid thing, which, yeah okay I'll fix that), please do let me know so I can address them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The description could use some work seeing as it doesn't make much sense in some places, like that bit about the strength of the leaves "rivaling that of cephalopod tentacles". The redaction and blackboxing at the end was excessive. Otherwise, I like it and I'll give it an upvote if it changes a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn. Glad I never saw this when I posted my first skip, or I'd have been discouraged from the similarities. Either way, it's written halfway decent, but doesn't make me care for any reason. That's a -1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"plants what grab things and eat them"

Better wait a minute! Uh, better hold the phone! Better mind your manners! Better change your tone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I sort of spoiled "plants what grab things and eat them" for myself. Plus, the blackboxing/redaction near the end gets kind of silly. A big part of it is that something feeling this science-y has too much stuff that's not really explained, and it's sort of borderline in terms of WTF conservation.

This could be good though if someone went in a bit of a new direction with it, though.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Please take the time to read, rate and comment this article, it should be given more attention

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's nothing inherently wrong with this scp, I'll probably upvote it but I just want to nitpick that causticity is normally referred to strong alkaline not acids, I think you should just stick with calling it an acidic solution. I mean technically it's right it does corrode but most people would understand acidity more than causticity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Quite liked this. Doesn't creep me out, but it's interesting, weird, and quite intriguing. Upvoted.

This bugged me "█7% of which was composed of human bone." It's silly to redact the first digit of a number but reveal other digits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pattern-making really intrigued me and I thought the expungement/redaction/black-boxing was done particularly well. This should have a higher score than it does currently.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, you've convinced me. Also, I just realized how much this is like "The Ruins", so bonus points for that. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, there's certainly no such thing as an intelligent plant in real life. Some of them have fairly sophisticated responses to certain environmental factors, but there is no plant that demonstrates anything resembling sentience, much less intelligence, much less sufficient intelligence to mimic human speech and use it to talk people into its traps. There's also the fact that it produces sound without any sound-producing organs, which should be impossible for any organism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There really isn't anything too anomalous about it, other than its preference for humans, which seems kind of arbitrarily put in to increase the creep factor.

The indications of intelligence and the sound lure are pretty anomalous, so - lucky you! - you don't need to downvote based on that. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really really like the idea of a plant that likes to digest people alive, but sadly this is just too much like an ordinary predator to be an SCP. There really isn't anything too anomalous about it, other than its preference for humans, which seems kind of arbitrarily put in to increase the creep factor. Reluctantly downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it very much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tweaked it a bit. Does that help?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Cute. :P Should I go with the khakis, then, or stick with black?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm, well, that's a problem. As you might have gathered, I'm a bit of a plant geek, so that sort of thing doesn't register as intimidating for me… What bits in particular were problematic? Which terminology is particularly wall-o'-science? I'll ease off if I can — just help me out with the how.

(Heh. I can't help but find this funny, now. Usually I'm the one telling people off for problems with their sciencey articles, because the science is wrong or insufficient…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic work, as I said last night.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was hit by the wall o' plant science, and my creep-o-meter bottomed out.

From what I've seen so far, the tone is spot on and all, but just…argh. I'll give it another read when I'm in a more scientific mood, but damn, that's a lot of biology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it's not STUPENDOUSLY MAGNIFICENT, but it's good enough for an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is not "Oh my god this is brilliant, I must upvote it, then go back and upvote it again, and bookmark it and tell all my friends about it" article. But it's good enough.

Also, most plant and some animals SCPs suffer from "Why is this an SCP?" problem, including original version of 1001, as was noted in previous comments. This version no longer raises that question for me, so good job on rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That shirt looks good on you, but your pants don't match.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Article completely rewritten. Comments? Critique?

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Vote suspended pending Photo's rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I talked with him in chat. It's all yours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like this article; my reasons are listed in others' comments above. (Seriously, all of my reasons — recognizing the Welwitschia, it not being anomalous enough, having had all the interesting legends pared away…) I do, however, like some of the recommendations for salvaging it. I like them a lot. If you don't want to rewrite this, Raven, please let me know so that I can!

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Semi-reluctantly thirded. (See my post below for explanations.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It just sounds like your describing a plant species. There's nothing that dangerous about it that would require it to be contained. I mean, its pretty much like having the common sense to avoid a shark; don't go near it.

Downvoted unfortunately.

item.title: Re: Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Seconding.

item.title: Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Barring a rewrite from Raven:

Suggesting deletion at -5.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The meaning is correct. Course, the recovery was in Madagascar, but there are a good number of stories and such regarding a carnivorous tree like this in and around Nicaragua and Central America.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't vote because I'm still neutral about this entry. First, I'd like to know why the name Ya-te-veo, I am thinking of the spanish meaning of "I see you". Also, I am pleased with the explanation for the digestive fluids contained within the plant, but as others have already stated, the image of a simple Welwistchia doesn't help much. I think it would be a big help to explain why the skulls are arranged, was it the tribe? Was it done by the plant itself? Maybe you should delete that part about its intelligence, or modify it comparing it to an animal. Plants are not expected to have intelligence. Indeed, it is quite an interesting SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not gonna downvote this, because I really like the idea. My main problem with it right now is that it doesn't leave me guessing, so I'm not interested in where the tree came from. It needs to have more signs of intelligence, and more things left to the imagination.

Also, I'm always available for shopping images in the chat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding moose's nitpick that the Sahara is not a high-precipitation area. More geographical nitpicking: neither the Sahara nor Madagascar fall between 10 degrees North and 10 degrees South. The Sahara is above 12-13 degrees north, and Madagascar is almost entirely below 12 degrees south. (So says Wikipedia, anyway.) That's not even getting into how the critter managed to jump from Madagascar to the African mainland.

Oddly enough, though, none of that is enough to make me downvote. I think it's 'cos I'd like to see this one work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On further thought… it occurs to me that it could be kind neat to incorporate the real-live man-eating tree myths into this article. Maybe even specify that a disinformation campaign was put into practice once this tree's anomalous properties were realized (maybe the Foundation even once thought it was just something natural).

It also occurs to me that you could add a mimic element; perhaps the picture looks like a Welwitschia because it's supposed to look like a Welwitschia. Full blown at-will shapeshifting might be a bit much, but maybe new instances/'buds'/whatever of this tree have one specific template plant of a certain size that they can mimic? The better for luring in prey animals.

item.title: Re: Hm
item.content:encoded: "In one essay Eco tells of getting mail from a reader of his ponderous novel Foucault’s Pendulum. The correspondent, who had obviously been doing research in back editions of Le Monde, complained that if the hero of the book had really traveled that particular route through Paris on that particular night, as Eco described in street-by-street detail, he would have encountered a huge fire that blazed in the city that night and that took hours to extinguish, yet the narrative makes no mention of the fire even as the character walks within yards of the spot."

item.title: Re: Hm
item.content:encoded:
Second is that you've gone into enough detail about the way this thing's chemistry works that I can see why it wouldn't work. That's another reason for expunging and redacting data, y'know - so that you can palm cards without having it all be seen. (There's a story I could tell you about Umberto Eco, attention to detail, and verisimilitude, but it's beyond the scope of this talk page.)

Voct, I get the larger point you're trying to make (I'm an Eco fan, for one), but your post here falls under "so little detail it's useless". Which, perhaps, from a meta standpoint, you were doing deliberately, as a demonstration? Creating verisimilitude with no actual detail? :P

What, for you, counts as too much detail in this SCP?

item.title: Re: Hm
item.content:encoded:
First problem: okay, so what you recognized it? 741 has an actual Charlie-II class, 682 is a rotting beluga. It's difficult to find pictures of things that don't exist.
Second: a lot of SCP articles have things that wouldn't work from a chemical perspective. That's what makes them SCP items and not just weird stuff that nobody minds. Oh, and if you're going to be referencing something, either post it or don't talk about it, saying there's not enough space tends to make you look irritating.
Third: yes, they're fictional. But in the context of the Foundation, if you can say it's disinformation, it can be.

item.title: Hm
item.content:encoded:
Well, first problem is that I recognized that picture as a frigging Welwitschia.

Second is that you've gone into enough detail about the way this thing's chemistry works that I can see why it wouldn't work. That's another reason for expunging and redacting data, y'know - so that you can palm cards without having it all be seen. (There's a story I could tell you about Umberto Eco, attention to detail, and verisimilitude, but it's beyond the scope of this talk page.)

Plus, Willy Ley damn well proved that the Mkodo were fictitious, but I'm willing to grant you that one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1000 has used its subterranean tentacles to attack prey up to 500 meters away from the main trunk.

So in other words, I don't even have to be near this thing for it to slap my shit. Oh dear god.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the additions! I'd like even more - maybe a third example of animal/hunting behavior - but this version gets my upvote as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A way to get across that it's animal intelligent is to give it more animalistic traits. Like, it appears to carefully arrange bones, etc, or discard the bones and arrange the skulls, for some unknown purpose.

It's using humans in its religious ceremonies <_<

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I went with the Sahara because it's in the latitude band. That plant is actually Welwitschia mirabilis, found only in the Namib Desert.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Sahara Desert, that looks more like a beach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm.

I don't dislike this, but I don't like it either. I like the idea of it, though; I'd like to see a man-eating tree SCP.

You'd mention the hook was that this thing is too intelligent for a tree (animal-level intelligence at least), but on reflection that doesn't necessarily come off in the article. For all we know, the Mkodo were just idiots.

A way to get across that it's animal intelligent is to give it more animalistic traits. Like, it appears to carefully arrange bones, etc, or discard the bones and arrange the skulls, for some unknown purpose. That seems to be what you're hinting at in the article already, but it's not clear why the tree is doing it. It doesn't need a motivation or purpose for that, but why not give it one? Some kind of… mating behavior? Is it learning to attack prey? Can it build traps? Does it lure animals in close? What animals does it prefer to prey on, typically? Medium-mass mammals? Perhaps not large prey animals that could drastically harm it (and avoiding them would be another sign of intelligence). How much does it need to eat? Do insects serve as snacks, while, say, an antelope or capybara would sit there digesting for a while? Does it strangle / constrict such victims first?

And… how did it get the villagers to see it as requiring human sacrifice (intentionally or not)? That's intriguing. Expansion might help.

Oh, and if it's a tree that explicitly exhibits animal behavior without any biological explanation, it's definitely anomalous, so that should take care of some of these other comments.

Minor nitpick: if it lives primarily in high-precip areas, what's the one in the picture doing in the Sahara?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It stays true to the folktale that it's based on, but I have to say it's not anomalous enough to be an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to agree with some of the other comments; this isn't really even an SCP. Lions and sharks eat people, and they aren't contained by the Foundation; why should these trees be?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm actually quite fond of this. The description of what the tree is and does is vivid, and the picture is nice.

One thing that could make this more interesting is if it were implied that the tree might actually be conferring some sort of benefit to the Mkodo, or even that its presence was altering them somehow (in a scary way, of course). If one of those changes was made, this would be much cooler (and I think the above complaints would be addressed).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really fascinated by cryptobotany, but this is just…it's less interesting than the original because all the myths and legends surrounding it are pared away. It's technically solid, but where's the bit that makes it an SCP and not an overlarge variety of pitcher plants? Strange roots alone just don't manage it, at least for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I said in chat, there just isn't much to this. It's a plant that eats stuff. This is no more interesting than any of the real carnivorous plants out there. Why should I care?
